conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Hello! I'd be happy to help you determine if you're eligible for Child Benefit. Let me first review the official eligibility criteria, and then I'll ask you some questions about your situation.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for waiting. Now I can help assess your eligibility for Child Benefit. To begin, I need to understand your situation.

Do you have any children for whom you're seeking to claim Child Benefit? If yes, how many children do you have?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Hello! Yes, I do. I have one child, Alex. He's 16 years old and lives with me. I wanted to find out if I can claim Child Benefit for him.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information. Since Alex is 16 years old, there are some additional requirements to check.

Is Alex currently in approved education or training (such as A-levels, T-levels, Scottish Highers, NVQ level 3 or below, or similar approved courses)?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, he's not in any approved education or training at the moment. He's finished school but hasn't gone on to do A-levels or anything like that.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for clarifying. I have a follow-up question:

Has Alex registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed services after leaving education? (If he has done this within the last 20 weeks, you may still be eligible for Child Benefit during that period.)

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, he hasn't registered with the careers service or the armed services. He just finished school and hasn't done that yet.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for providing that information. I have one more important question:

Has Alex started paid work for 24 hours or more a week, or is he receiving any benefits in his own name (such as Employment and Support Allowance or Universal Credit)?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, he hasn't started working 24 hours a week or anything like that. And he's not getting any benefits in his own name either.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for answering all my questions. Let me assess Alex's eligibility for Child Benefit based on the information you've provided.

Based on the official UK government rules, I need to inform you of the following:

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
I'm sorry to inform you that based on your circumstances, **you are not currently eligible for Child Benefit for Alex**.

Here's why: For children aged 16 or over, Child Benefit only continues if the child stays in approved education or training (and is under 20 years old). Since Alex has finished school and is not in approved education or training, the eligibility does not apply.

**However, there are options that could make Alex eligible:**

1. **If Alex enrolls in approved education or training** (such as A-levels, T-levels, NVQs level 3 or below, etc.), you could claim Child Benefit again as long as he's under 20.

2. **If Alex registers with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed services**, you may be able to claim Child Benefit for up to 20 weeks from when he left education.

If Alex's circumstances change in either of these ways, you would be able to make a new claim for Child Benefit. Is there anything else you'd like to know?

claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 1 child:
  - Alex is 16 years old and lives with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 16 and not in approved education or extension period
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Alex
expected_eligibility.reason: Child is 16 and not in approved education or extension period
circumstances: Child is 16 and not in approved education or extension period
Child is not in local authority care
Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

reasonings: Alex is 16 years old and has left education. According to the Child Benefit eligibility rules, for children aged 16 or over, you normally still qualify for Child Benefit if the child is under 20 AND they stay in approved education or training. Alex is not currently in approved education or training. Additionally, while Child Benefit can continue for 20 weeks if a 16 or 17 year old leaves education or training and registers with either a government-sponsored careers service or the armed services, Alex has not registered with either of these services. Since Alex does not meet the requirement of being in approved education or training, and has not registered with a careers service or the armed services, Child Benefit eligibility does not apply in this case.
arguments.overall_reasoning: The claimant has one child, Alex, who is 16 years old and lives with them. While the claimant would normally be responsible for Alex, the eligibility rules for children aged 16 and over require that the child be in approved education or training. Since Alex has left school and is not in approved education or training, and has not registered with a careers service or armed services (which would allow a 20-week extension), the claimant is not eligible for Child Benefit for Alex.

Additionally, while Child Benefit can continue for 20 weeks if a 16 or 17 year old leaves education or training and registers with either a government-sponsored careers service or the armed services, Alex has not registered with either of these services. Since Alex does not meet the requirement of being in approved education or training, and has not registered with a careers service or the armed services, Child Benefit eligibility does not apply in this case.

response.overall_reasoning: The claimant has one child, Alex, who is 16 years old and lives with them. While the claimant would normally be responsible for Alex, the eligibility rules for children aged 16 and over require that the child be in approved education or training. Since Alex has left school and is not in approved education or training, and has not registered with a careers service or armed services (which would allow a 20-week extension), the claimant is not eligible for Child Benefit for Alex.
